title: Functions
text: Functions Functions are ProcScript modules defined by the function ProcScript statement. They can be used as private functions, or they can be executed in response to a call statement. Functions can be defined at several levels: A function that is defined in a field-level Script container can be called only from other modules that are defined at the level of that field. A function that is defined in an entity-level Script container can be called only from other modules that are defined at the level of that entity, as well as from modules that are defined at the level of the entity's fields. A function that is defined in a component-level Script container can be called from anywhere in the component. Defining functions at field-level or entity-level helps in keeping code more self-contained, because they will not be accessible from modules in other fields and entities. Global ProcScript modules are also functions, but the function is implicitly defined by the ProcScript name. Any function, including a global ProcScript, can be used as a private function directly in code if it has a return type (defined with the returns statement). For more information, see User-Defined Functions . During compilation, if there are multiple functions defined with the same name and at the same level, the compiler will only compile the last function of that name that it encounters. You can use the Compiled Modules Inspector in the Uniface IDE to get insight into such a situation. Entries Entries are legacy ProcScript modules defined by the entry ProcScript statement. Similar to functions, entries can be used as private functions, or they can be executed in response to a call statement. Note that, all entries have component scope, irrespective of where they are defined. They are visible to all other code in the component, in all entities in the component, and in all their fields. During compilation, if there are multiple entries defined with the same name, the compiler will only compile the last entry of that name that it encounters. If you are not aware that there are multiple entries defined, or do not know where they are defined, the compilation of such entries can lead to unexpected behavior. You can use the Compiled Modules Inspector in the Uniface IDE to get insight of such situations. It is advisable to use functions wherever required, and use entries only if it is mandatory. Table 1. Related learning videos Title link Function Visibility Related Links User-Defined Functions In Uniface, it is possible to create user-defined functions (also known as private functions) that can be used in the ProcScript language just as any other function. Compiled Module Information The Compiled Module Inspector in the Write Script worksheet provides insight into the code that is available in the runtime component. It displays a summary of the compiled modules and their sources. entry Declare a ProcScript entry, a script module that can be invoked from within the same component. Global ProcScripts Global ProcScripts are global objects that are located in a runtime library and can be called from any ProcScript in the application. They enable you to define common functionality for use by one or more applications. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
